name: Bodyweight Squat
description: A foundational lower-body movement that teaches proper squat mechanics. Perfect for beginners to learn joint alignment and knee tracking before adding weight. This exercise builds strength in your legs and glutes while improving mobility.
tips: Keep your feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out
Lower down as if sitting in a chair, pushing hips back
Keep knees aligned with your toes - avoid letting them collapse inward
Maintain a neutral spine - don't round your back
Push through your heels to return to standing
Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout
commonMistakes: Knees collapsing inward (valgus collapse)
Rounding the lower back
Heels lifting off the ground
Knees tracking too far forward past toes

name: Deadlift
description: A fundamental posterior chain exercise that teaches the hip hinge pattern. Essential for building glutes, hamstrings, and lower back strength. Perfect for learning proper spine alignment and posterior chain activation.
tips: Start with feet hip-width apart, bar close to shins
Hinge at the hips, pushing hips back while keeping chest up
Maintain a neutral spine throughout - no rounding
Keep the bar close to your body as you lift
Drive through your heels and squeeze glutes at the top
Lower with control, maintaining spine alignment
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back (spinal flexion)
Lifting with rounded shoulders
Bar drifting away from body
Not engaging glutes and hamstrings properly

name: Push-Up
description: A classic upper-body push pattern that strengthens chest, triceps, and core. Can be easily modified for beginners (knee or wall push-ups) or progressed for advanced users. Great for learning proper body alignment and controlled movement.
tips: Start in plank position with hands directly under shoulders
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Lower your body until chest nearly touches the floor
Push back up to starting position with control
Engage your core throughout to maintain body alignment
For beginners: try knee push-ups or wall push-ups
For progression: elevate feet or add tempo variations
commonMistakes: Sagging hips or lifting hips too high
Hands too wide or too narrow
Not going through full range of motion
Head dropping forward

name: Bicep Curl
description: A simple isolation movement perfect for beginners to understand controlled, focused motion. Teaches proper form for arm exercises and helps build bicep strength and muscle mind connection.
tips: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart
Keep elbows close to your sides
Curl weight up with control, squeezing biceps at top
Lower slowly with control - don't let gravity do the work
Avoid swinging or using momentum
Keep your core engaged and shoulders relaxed
commonMistakes: Swinging the weight using momentum
Moving elbows away from body
Not controlling the negative (lowering) phase
Using too much weight causing form breakdown

name: Side Lying Leg Raise
description: A beginner-friendly hip-strength exercise that targets the glutes and hip abductors. By moving the top leg while keeping your pelvis stable, you learn how to activate the muscles on the outside of your hips without compensating.
tips: Lie on your side with your bottom leg slightly bent for support
Stack shoulders and hips (don’t rotate forward or backward)
Keep your pelvis steady as you lift the top leg a few inches
Point toes slightly forward (comfortable angle) and control the lift
Pause briefly at the top, then lower with control
Breathe steadily and keep your ribs down (avoid flaring)
Complete reps slowly on one side before switching
commonMistakes: Rolling your torso or letting your pelvis twist during the lift
Lifting too high and losing control (moving from your back instead of hips)
Allowing the bottom leg to collapse inward
Rushing reps and bouncing at the bottom
modifications: Reduce range of motion: lift only 2-3 inches
Keep the bottom leg bent and more supportive
Use a wall or pillow behind your back to help prevent torso rotation
amplifications: Lift higher with straightening the top leg slightly (only if pelvis stays stable)
Add ankle weight or a light resistance band around your ankles
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds before lowering

name: Lunge
description: A single-leg stability exercise that improves balance, coordination, and leg strength. Great for addressing muscle imbalances and teaching proper front/back plane movement. Challenges each leg independently.
tips: Step forward with one leg into a split stance
Lower your hips until both knees are bent at 90 degrees
Keep front knee aligned with ankle, not past toes
Back knee should hover just above the ground
Keep torso upright, core engaged
Push through front heel to return to starting position
Alternate legs for balanced training
commonMistakes: Front knee collapsing inward
Front knee tracking too far forward
Leaning torso too far forward
Not maintaining balance and stability

name: Glute Bridge
description: A beginner-friendly posterior chain exercise that's perfect for learning glute activation. Low intimidation factor makes it ideal for those new to strength training. Great for highlighting muscle activation and teaching hip extension.
tips: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on floor
Place feet hip-width apart, close to glutes
Engage your glutes and lift hips up
Create a straight line from knees to shoulders
Squeeze glutes at the top, hold for 1-2 seconds
Lower with control
Focus on feeling glutes do the work, not lower back
commonMistakes: Overarching lower back instead of using glutes
Feet too far from glutes
Not fully engaging glutes
Rushing through the movement

name: Bicycle Crunch
description: A dynamic core movement that trains the rectus abdominis and obliques through alternating elbow-to-knee motion. It also reinforces coordinated control and breathing for safer trunk rotation.
tips: Lie on your back with hands lightly supporting behind your head (don’t pull on your neck)
Lift shoulders off the floor and keep your ribs pulled down
Bring one elbow toward the opposite knee while extending the other leg
Keep your twist controlled: move from your trunk, not your neck
Breathe out as you crunch and slow down if your form breaks
Make reps smooth and consistent—no jerking
Move through a comfortable range first, then build range
commonMistakes: Pulling with your arms and yanking your head forward
Rushing reps so you lose control and coordination
Letting your lower back over-arch or your ribs flare
Twisting too far and losing the crunch focus
modifications: Start with smaller range: bring elbow toward knee without fully extending the legs
Keep both feet closer to the floor to reduce strain
Slow the tempo: 1 rep = 2 seconds up, 2 seconds down
amplifications: Extend legs farther to increase lever length (only if your ribs stay down)
Increase tempo slightly while maintaining smooth control
Pause briefly at the crunch peak for extra core engagement

name: Pull-Up / Assisted Pull-Up
description: An upper-body pull pattern that builds back, lat, and arm strength. Shows the concept of progression - start with assisted variations and work toward full pull-ups. An aspirational skill that motivates users to build strength over time.
tips: Start with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width
Hang with arms fully extended, engage your core
Pull your body up until chin clears the bar
Lower with control - don't drop down
For beginners: use resistance bands or assisted pull-up machine
Focus on pulling with your back muscles, not just arms
Keep shoulders down and away from ears
commonMistakes: Swinging or using momentum (kipping)
Not going through full range of motion
Shrugging shoulders up
Not engaging back muscles properly
progression: Start with dead hangs to build grip strength

name: Crunch
description: A foundational ab exercise that teaches controlled spinal flexion and core engagement. Crunches help beginners understand how to lift the ribcage with tension in the core, building a safer base for more advanced ab movements.
tips: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor
Keep your hands lightly on your head or crossed over your chest
Exhale and lift your shoulders off the floor slightly (don’t pull hard on your neck)
Think “chest up” instead of yanking yourself forward
Pause briefly at the top with control
Inhale as you lower back down to the start position
Keep the movement small and precise at first
commonMistakes: Pulling your neck with your hands
Using momentum instead of controlled crunches
Lifting too high and turning it into a sit-up
Rushing reps and losing core tension
modifications: Reduce range: lift shoulders only a few inches
Keep feet closer to your body for easier control
Slow down: 2 seconds up, 2 seconds down
amplifications: Pause at the top for 1-2 seconds before lowering
Increase range slightly while keeping ribs tucked
Add light resistance (e.g., hold a light plate or dumbbell at chest level) if form stays clean
